title: What Comes After The Refugee Camp | NOEMA
gist: Craze argues the post-1951 global refugee regime is collapsing as Global North aid budgets implode, replacing humanitarian camps with a worldwide archipelago of detention centers and externalized borders—just as climate displacement is set to quadruple, making the spending shift structural rather than cyclical.

insights.claim: After the US suspended foreign assistance, 100,000 residents of Kenya's Kakuma camp lost humanitarian support, part of a multi-year decline where WFP cut per-refugee aid from $17/month (2023) to $13, then to $5 in 2025.
insights.speaker: Joshua Craze
insights.so_what: Quantifies the speed of the aid drawdown; a leading indicator that NGO-aid-dependent regional economies and aid-logistics contractors face a structural revenue cliff.
insights.quote: In 2025, WFP slashed support to $5 a month.
insights.locator: After U.S. President Donald Trump abruptly suspended foreign assistance

insights.claim: The aid retreat is broad-based across the Global North—the US, EU, France and Germany all cut humanitarian funding, with the UK in 2025–26 slashing foreign assistance proportionately even more aggressively than America.
insights.speaker: Joshua Craze
insights.so_what: Signals the cut is a coordinated structural shift across donor governments, not a one-off US policy reversible by an election—plan for durable austerity in aid markets.
insights.quote: U.S. reductions in humanitarian funding followed similar cuts by the European Union (EU), France and Germany. Proportionately, the U.K. slashed foreign assistance in 2025–26 even more aggressively than America did.
insights.locator: Humanitarianism has been in retreat for some time

insights.claim: The funding implosion forced UNHCR to lay off 30% of its workforce and cut assistance to 13 million refugees, while IOM let 6,000 staff go as its budget fell 25% in 2025 and contracted further in 2026.
insights.speaker: Joshua Craze
insights.so_what: The core institutions of the 1950s refugee regime are downsizing in lockstep—a measurable contraction of the multilateral humanitarian apparatus.
insights.quote: The U.N. refugee agency, the UNHCR, laid off 30% of its workforce and cut assistance to 13 million refugees.
insights.locator: Across the world, humanitarian operations in refugee camps were plunged

insights.claim: WFP, heavily dependent on US funds, removed all food assistance for a million refugees in Uganda and cut rations by up to 80% for another 800,000 after the cuts.
insights.speaker: Joshua Craze
insights.so_what: Illustrates concentration risk—single-donor dependence converts a US budget decision directly into mass food insecurity, raising regional instability and migration pressure.
insights.quote: It removed all food assistance for a million refugees in Uganda, while another 800,000 had their rations reduced by up to 80%.
insights.locator: In Rohingya settlements near Cox’s Bazar

insights.claim: Even before Trump's cuts, the detention center had already replaced the refugee camp as the dominant policy priority, with border enforcement consuming vastly more capital than feeding the displaced.
insights.speaker: Joshua Craze
insights.so_what: Public spending is rotating from humanitarian aid to border security and detention—the structural beneficiaries are surveillance, detention and border-enforcement contractors.
insights.quote: Even before Trump’s cuts, the detention center had replaced the refugee camp as a policy priority.
insights.locator: European funds frequently flow to “migration management” instead.

insights.claim: The EU has externalized its borders deep into North Africa, signing a $6.6 billion deal with Turkey in 2016 and by 2018 treating Niger, Libya and Sudan as 'Europe's new southern border,' funding militias, drones and digital surveillance to block movement.
insights.speaker: Joshua Craze
insights.so_what: Large recurring EU outlays to third-country security forces define a durable spending stream around border externalization—watch for who supplies the drones and surveillance networks.
insights.quote: The EU signed a $6.6 billion deal with Turkey in 2016 to keep Syrian refugees on its soil.
insights.locator: As the Syrian refugee crisis worsened in 2015–16

insights.claim: Europe now operates more than 400 detention centers and a 21st-century fortress 'constructed from drones and digital surveillance networks,' while a new EU Migration and Asylum Pact (effective June 12) detains irregular arrivals and strips asylum safeguards.
insights.speaker: Joshua Craze
entities: European Pact on Migration and Asylum
insights.so_what: Codifies detention-and-deportation as default EU policy—structural demand for detention infrastructure, surveillance tech and deportation logistics across the continent.
insights.quote: there are now more than 400 detention centers ready to greet them
insights.locator: If refugees manage to escape the detention centers

insights.claim: Border hardening reroutes rather than reduces migration: externalizing frontiers into North Africa surged the Senegal-to-Canary-Islands route, and hardening Morocco's borders skyrocketed departures along the riskier Mauritania Atlantic route.
insights.speaker: Joshua Craze
insights.so_what: Enforcement displaces flows instead of stopping them—implies open-ended escalation of border spending with diminishing returns, a recurring fiscal commitment.
insights.quote: When the EU externalized its frontiers into North Africa, the migrant sea route from Senegal to the Canary Islands saw traffic surge.
insights.locator: As popular as these policies may be

insights.claim: Migratory flows into Europe are fundamentally driven by geopolitical conditions—wars and catastrophes—rather than by EU border policy, so barbed wire and drones cannot change the underlying number of displaced.
insights.speaker: Joshua Craze
insights.so_what: Frames border-enforcement spending as structurally ineffective against root drivers—a policy that persists for electoral reasons, not efficacy, and will keep absorbing capital.
insights.quote: The global number of the displaced changes due to wars and catastrophes, not barbed wire and drones.
insights.locator: All these schemes ignore the fact

insights.claim: By end-2025, 117 million people were displaced worldwide (down slightly from a record 123 million in 2024), versus just 38 million in 2000, with Sudan's war the largest displacement crisis.
insights.speaker: Joshua Craze
insights.so_what: A roughly threefold rise in displacement since 2000 sets a rising structural baseline of demand exactly as funding shrinks—a widening gap that drives instability.
insights.quote: By the end of 2025, 117 million people were displaced worldwide, a slight decrease from a world record 123 million in 2024
insights.locator: The crisis in the global refugee regime does not mean

insights.claim: The number of people displaced by climate catastrophe is projected to quadruple by 2050, yet under the 1951 Refugee Convention climate-displaced people are not accorded refugee status.
insights.speaker: Joshua Craze
insights.so_what: A legal vacuum around a quadrupling climate-migration wave means no funded framework to absorb it—structural pressure on borders, host states and instability premia through 2050.
insights.quote: the number of people displaced by climate catastrophe is projected to quadruple by 2050.
insights.locator: Many have already left, and many more will follow.

insights.claim: Even the modest 1951 Convention would not be ratified today, as states are more concerned with border sanctity than humane treatment of displacement, leaving international institutions answerable to reactionary nation-states.
insights.speaker: Joshua Craze
insights.so_what: Signals no political appetite to expand the legal regime—climate migration will be managed through enforcement, not protection, locking in the detention-spending trajectory.
insights.quote: even the modest clauses of the 1951 Convention would not be ratified today by states more concerned with the sanctity of their borders
insights.locator: Some scholars have advocated for an expanded agreement

insights.claim: The refugee camp has always functioned simultaneously as a cage built to control inhabitants and a hospital meant to keep them on life support; without sustaining funds, camps become only cages designed to prevent border crossing.
insights.speaker: Joshua Craze
insights.so_what: A dual-function frame explaining why funding collapse converts containment-with-care into pure containment—the core mechanism behind rising instability in host regions.
insights.quote: The refugee camp has always functioned as both a cage, constructed to control its inhabitants, and a hospital, intended to keep its patients on life support.
insights.locator: Reductions in funding had such terrible consequences

insights.claim: Camps designed as temporary stopgaps have become permanent fixtures because conflicts outlast states—children born in Kenya's Dadaab and Kakuma in the 1990s are now in their thirties with no right to citizenship or political participation.
insights.speaker: Joshua Craze
insights.so_what: Demonstrates the irreversibility of displacement infrastructure—'temporary' commitments become multi-decade fiscal and political liabilities for host states and donors.
insights.quote: What was intended as a stopgap has become a permanent fixture of our world.
insights.locator: That was 40 years ago.

insights.claim: The humanitarian sector ballooned in the 1980s on a now-defunct theory that aid would stabilize emergencies until development agencies built functional states—but humanitarian emergencies have instead become permanent.
insights.speaker: Joshua Craze
insights.so_what: The foundational rationale for aid spending has broken, undermining donor willingness to fund open-ended 'palliative care'—structural justification for the budget retreat.
insights.quote: Humanitarian emergencies have often become permanent, as countries like Sudan remain mired in conflict
insights.locator: The humanitarian sector ballooned in the 1980s

insights.claim: Camp governance has always been intertwined with and dependent on coercive state power, with aid workers in Kakuma relying on the hard edge of the Kenyan police force to control the very people they serve.
insights.speaker: Joshua Craze
insights.so_what: Reveals humanitarianism and state coercion as inseparable—relevant to ESG/reputational risk for institutions funding or operating in camp systems.
insights.quote: aid workers have relied on the hard edge of the Kenyan police force
insights.locator: Refugee camp governance has always been intertwined

insights.claim: The 2016 Jordan Compact—drawing on Betts and Collier's plan to put refugees to work in exchange for EU grants and work permits—failed at every level, drawing only 11 participating companies two years in.
insights.speaker: Joshua Craze
insights.so_what: A cautionary case that 'refugees-as-labor' special economic zones fail commercially—skepticism warranted for similar donor-funded development schemes.
insights.quote: The Jordan Compact failed at every level.
insights.locator: One scheme that warns against optimistic readings

insights.claim: Kenya's Shirika Plan is redesignating Dadaab and Kakuma as municipalities at a cost of around a billion dollars largely funded by the World Bank, but in arid landscapes hostile to agriculture and dependent on collapsing aid, viability is doubtful.
insights.speaker: Joshua Craze
insights.so_what: A billion-dollar World Bank-funded urbanization bet on economically marginal sites; flags execution risk in donor-led refugee-city development.
insights.quote: The redesignation, including infrastructure construction, will cost around a billion dollars, largely funded by the World Bank.
insights.locator: In 2025, both Dadaab and Kakuma were redesignated as municipalities

insights.claim: Kakuma, before the Trump-cuts crisis, had a camp economy of $56 million, showing that even aid-dependent camps generate substantial localized economic activity.
insights.speaker: Joshua Craze
insights.so_what: Quantifies the local economic footprint at risk from aid cuts—a measurable shock to host-county economies dependent on the camp economy.
insights.quote: Kakuma, at least before the economic crisis produced by Trump’s cuts, had an economy of $56 million.
insights.locator: In 2021, the Kenyan government passed a Refugee Act

insights.claim: Both the Jordan Compact and Kenya's Shirika Plan suspend questions of citizenship and political rights, so camps may end up looking like cities but remain cages—reproducing the core flaw of humanitarian rule.
insights.speaker: Joshua Craze
insights.so_what: Argues market-and-labor reforms without political rights cannot create stable, productive settlements—predicts recurring failure of the dominant 'solution' model.
insights.quote: The camps may end up looking like cities, but they would nonetheless remain cages.
insights.locator: While it doesn’t treat the camps’ residents as pure victims

insights.claim: UNHCR's three durable solutions—return, resettlement, integration—have collapsed: return is impossible when states exist only on maps, resettlement reached just 188,800 of 42.7 million refugees in 2024, and host-government hostility blocks integration.
insights.speaker: Joshua Craze
insights.so_what: With every exit path foreclosed, the displaced population can only accumulate—an inexorable structural buildup of stranded people and associated instability.
insights.quote: In 2024, 188,800 of a total of 42.7 million refugees globally were resettled in a third country.
insights.locator: Resettlement in a foreign country, UNHCR’s second preferred option

insights.claim: UNHCR has become a proxy for its principal donors, training and equipping the Libyan coast guard since 2015 and policing Greece's detention centers, narrowing its three solutions to two—forced return or indefinite detention.
insights.speaker: Joshua Craze
insights.so_what: The protection mandate has inverted into enforcement—multilateral aid bodies now function as instruments of border control, a reputational and mission-integrity reversal.
insights.quote: UNHCR has today become a proxy for its principal donors, which wish to block refugee movement, not enable it.
insights.locator: Today’s UNHCR is in an unenviable position.

insights.claim: IOM is paid to deport: in Greece it oversees an 'Assisted Voluntary Return' program and receives a cash bonus for every refugee it deports, while in Panama it assists US self-deportations.
insights.speaker: Joshua Craze
insights.so_what: A per-deportation payment model embeds financial incentives into a UN agency's return programs—an ethical and governance red flag in the humanitarian-industrial complex.
insights.quote: In Greece, IOM oversees the “Assisted Voluntary Return and Reintegration” program and receives a cash bonus for every refugee it deports.
insights.locator: Elsewhere, U.N. agencies find themselves on the soft side

insights.claim: The cruelty of Europe's asylum legislation is set by electoral calculus, with politicians competing to be toughest on migration as anti-migrant sentiment proves a reliable vote-winner.
insights.speaker: Joshua Craze
insights.so_what: Anti-migration policy is electorally self-reinforcing—structurally durable across the political spectrum regardless of efficacy, anchoring the long-term border-spending trend.
insights.quote: The cruelty of Europe’s asylum legislation today is determined by an electoral calculus, as politicians across the continent vie to be the toughest on migration.
insights.locator: This attempt to block refugees from entering Europe

insights.claim: Germany's political reversal is stark: the far-right AfD won almost 21% in February 2025, and by March 2026 Chancellor Merz urged Syrian refugees to return—threatening deportation—even though two-thirds of Syria remains aid-dependent.
insights.speaker: Joshua Craze
insights.so_what: The continent's former asylum anchor has flipped to deportation—signals the rightward policy ratchet is now mainstream, not fringe, across major EU economies.
insights.quote: The Alternative für Deutschland — a far-right party that wants to effectively end asylum as a right — won almost 21% of the vote in the February 2025 national election.
insights.locator: The rightward tilt of global politics

insights.claim: Host governments in North Africa harden refugee policies in direct response to the promise of EU funding, and made the camps profitable by taxing aid workers and providing logistics.
insights.speaker: Joshua Craze
insights.so_what: EU payments actively incentivize harsher third-country enforcement—a transactional model that turns migration control into a revenue stream for partner states.
insights.quote: Host governments in North Africa have hardened their policies toward refugees in response to the promise of EU funding
insights.locator: Even before the pandemic and the accession of Trump 2.0
